does: This paper introduces a systematic approach to improving the aesthetic formatting and visual presentation of text generated by large language models. The authors create TexAes, a dataset of aesthetically polished textual responses, and propose TAPO (textual aesthetics-powered direct preference optimization), a fine-tuning method that optimizes for both aesthetic quality—including layout cleanliness, consistency, and coherence—and content correctness. They develop two complementary evaluation methods for assessing textual aesthetics: one based on textual features and another leveraging image-based analysis of rendered text. Experimental results show that training with their method improves aesthetic scores while maintaining or enhancing performance on general benchmarks, demonstrating that aesthetic optimization and content quality are not mutually exclusive objectives.
connects: This work extends the emerging field of computational aesthetics, previously well-developed for images, into the domain of text generation. It builds on recent advances in preference-based fine-tuning methods, particularly direct preference optimization (DPO), which has shown success in aligning LLM outputs with human preferences beyond traditional supervised learning. The paper responds to the observation that while LLM research has extensively addressed content correctness and helpfulness, the presentation quality of responses remains understudied. It connects to broader efforts in LLM alignment and controllable generation that seek to optimize multiple dimensions of output quality simultaneously, drawing parallels with work on multi-objective optimization in neural text generation.
enables: This work opens new research directions in LLM output quality beyond semantic correctness. It enables development of more comprehensive evaluation frameworks that consider user experience holistically, including how information is visually presented. The textual aesthetics dataset and methodology could support future work on controllable generation where models produce outputs tailored to specific formatting preferences or accessibility requirements. This foundation could facilitate research into how presentation aesthetics affect user comprehension, engagement, and trust in LLM outputs. Additionally, the dual evaluation approach combining textual and image-based analysis could inspire similar methodologies for other implicit quality dimensions in generated text, advancing toward LLMs that optimize for full-spectrum user experience rather than correctness alone.
